Document [0] (Title: Acute Stress Disorder -- Treatment / Management) Psychotherapy - Treatment of choice for acute stress disorder (ASD) is a unique form of cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) called trauma-focused CBT. CBT can reduce the risk of further developing PTSD. [22] This evidence-based practice can be delivered via the internet, in person, or by phone. It focuses on increasing knowledge on trauma psychology, symptom management skills, identifying and disputing cognitive distortions, and exposure therapy. Exposure therapy is a CBT method that involves controlled patient exposure to the traumatic source to relieve the trauma memory mimicking fear extinction (discussed in the pathophysiology section). Exposure therapy is the standard of care for ASD (and PTSD). A transient worsening of the symptoms can appear, but it is not more common than other intervention methods. [2] Debriefing-which involves asking the patient to explain in detail, the trauma and their feeling about it in the first 72 hours is widely available. studies have not shown the efficacy of debriefing in preventing the development of PTSD, and debriefing is discouraged from routine administration to ASD patients. Document [15] (Title: Systolic Hypertension -- Treatment / Management -- Nonpharmacologic Therapy) Recommended for all patients, regardless of the reading of the blood pressure. Restrict dietary salt (aim for 1.5 g or less per day). Randomized controlled trials have shown that moderate sodium reduction can result in an average blood pressure reduction of 4.8/2.5 mmHg. Weight loss (every 1 pound or 0.45 kg of weight loss will result in a reduction of 1 mmHg blood pressure). Start dietary approaches to stop hypertension (DASH) diet (rich in fruits, vegetables, whole grains, fish, and low-fat dairy products). Aerobic and resistance training can reduce blood pressure by an average of 4/3 to 6/3 mm Hg, irrespective of body weight. Document [16] (Title: Adlerian Therapy -- Function -- Lifestyle) Lifestyle or style of life, the Adlerian terminology for personality, is a personal metanarrative that involves an individual's particular beliefs, purposes, and principles for dealing with the adversities of living. This life plan is distinctive for each person and emerges early in life, approximately when the individual reaches 6 years of age. Lifestyle consists of our comprehension of events rather than the events themselves, profoundly influencing our personalities. This notion includes self-concept, self-ideals, views of the world, and ethical convictions. Later, this cognitive orientation is molded within the initial social setting of the family constellation. The family constellation consists of family atmosphere, values, and gender lines. [7] These aspects influence an individual's lifestyle and determine how we act, think, perceive, or live. We select methods for enduring life challenges and cues from developing lifestyles. According to the Adlerian theory, humans are proactive and creative in developing their lifestyle, functioning as playwrights and actors while constructing their personalities within a socially embedded context. Adler outlined 4 basic lifestyles: the socially useful, the ruling type, the getting type, and the avoiding type. [8] The "socially useful" is also described as a responsible lifestyle. This category includes individuals who are the healthiest, most complete, and most cheerful typology. They possess a strong sense of social responsibility and strive daily to improve themselves, overcome their worries, build resilience, and eliminate greed. Socially useful individuals tend to be mature, optimistic, well-adjusted, polite, and respectful of others. They do not aim for superiority but to solve adversities in ways that are cognizant of others. [9] The "ruling type" is also described as a selfish lifestyle. Individuals with limited social interests attempt to demonstrate authority and seek dominance over others. These people tend to be confrontational and possess harsh energy that subjugates anyone who gets in their way. They adopt an antisocial approach to society. The most energetic of these are intimidators and sadists. Meanwhile, less active individuals turn their power inward and hurt themselves, such as individuals with substance use disorders and suicidal ideation. [9] The "getting type" is also known as a dependent lifestyle. These individuals conduct their existence in one direction: obtaining reinforcements from others. They usually want to receive everything from others without any effort or struggle. They have low social interests and non-reciprocity. The main explanation for their behavior is that they have not overcome their feelings of inferiority. Consequently, they develop relationships of dependence and absolute submission. [9] The "avoiding type" is also recognized as an escapist lifestyle. These people try to dodge duties, evade responsibilities, and cannot maintain relationships. They display childish behaviors, pursue an easy life, and experience immediate satisfaction. They tend to have the lowest energy and prefer unrealistic daydreaming and being fanciful instead of facing reality. Additionally, individuals of this type demonstrate little social contact because of fear of rejection, evading life in general and others in particular. [9]

New York: Guilford). [23] Guided practices, including the body scan and sitting meditations, are available on electronic media or for download from several sources. There is also an abundance of freely available apps and podcasts that offer mindfulness teachings, guided and unguided timed sitting and supine meditations, and guided mindfulness practices to listen to during activities such as exercising or cleaning. Most are available for free or for a range of modest costs. Some offer free trials, while some apps and many quality podcasts are totally free. Encourage interested individuals to try out a few different options and find what works best for them. Practitioners also may find that different approaches are more preferable, depending on the day, stress level, and practice time available. We caution individuals to avoid listening to guided sessions or podcasts while driving, as increased drowsiness may occur.

Document [25] (Title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2868) Abnormal chest X‑ray findings (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية) are clinically associated with alcohol consumption (تعاطي الكحول). In patients who consume alcohol, clinicians should consider that pulmonary imaging may reveal atypical or pathological changes, which can stem from alcohol‑related lung injury, aspiration events, infections, or other respiratory complications. Recognizing this association prompts healthcare providers to inquire about alcohol use when interpreting abnormal chest radiographs, to assess for potential alcohol‑related pulmonary disease, and to incorporate appropriate counseling or treatment for alcohol misuse into the overall patient care plan.
Document [26] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2212) Citalopram is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor prescribed for depressive and anxiety disorders. Alcohol dependence (الاعتماد على الكحول) is a substance‑use disorder that can interfere with the effectiveness and safety of citalopram, rather than serve as a treatment for it. Clinicians should be aware that patients with alcohol dependence may require careful monitoring, dose adjustments, or alternative therapeutic strategies when using citalopram.
Document [27] (Title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 5 -- Community 2962) Acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد) is clinically associated with type 2 diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري نوع 2) and with reduced blood volume (قلة حجم الدم). Type 2 diabetes can predispose patients to acute kidney injury through chronic hyperglycemia‑induced vascular and tubular damage, making the kidneys more vulnerable to hemodynamic stress. Conversely, hypovolemia decreases renal perfusion and can directly precipitate acute kidney failure. Recognizing that both type 2 diabetes and low intravascular volume are important clinical contributors to acute kidney failure guides clinicians to monitor diabetic patients closely, ensure adequate hydration, and intervene promptly to preserve renal function.
Document [28] (Title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1864) Alcohol-related disorders are clinically associated with a confusional state (حالة ارتباكية) and are also linked to hallucinations (الهَلْوَسة). Management of alcohol-related disorders may include the use of benzodiazepine agents such as clorazepate and prazepam, which are considered potential treatments for the symptoms and complications of these disorders.

This relationship indicates that patients experiencing alcohol abstinence may also develop or have concurrent liver inflammation, underscoring the importance of monitoring hepatic function and providing appropriate liver‑focused care during the management of alcohol withdrawal.
Document [30] (Title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1882) Alcohol-related disorders are linked to several therapeutic and clinical considerations. Oxazepam and clorazepate are identified as medications that may be used to treat alcohol-related disorders, reflecting the role of benzodiazepines in managing withdrawal and related symptoms. Clinically, alcohol-related disorders are associated with حالة ارتباكية, indicating a frequent presentation of confusion or altered mental status in affected patients. The condition also has a noted relationship with الهَلْوَسة, suggesting an additional clinical or cultural manifestation tied to these disorders. Importantly, beta-D-glucopyranose is contraindicated in the context of alcohol-related disorders, highlighting that this compound should be avoided in patients with this condition to prevent adverse effects. Together, these connections underscore the importance of appropriate pharmacologic selection, awareness of neurocognitive complications, and avoidance of contraindicated substances in the care of individuals with alcohol-related disorders.
